text: wholeheartedly to academia. 1908 was also the year that van Gennep founded the scientific Journal, La Revue des Études Ethnographiques et Sociologiques, in which he would publish with frequency, while also functioning as Directeur and administrator. In 1908 he also published a smaller book on Homer and Homeric poetry (La question d’Homère); and he published the first volume of Religions, mæurs et legends. Essais d’ethnographie et de linguistique, a collection of his best essays on religion, myth and ritual, which was positively reviewed in a series of international journals. In 1909 the second volume of Religions, mæurs et legends came out (still with Mercure de France). The flow of articles, reviews and translations continued. In 1910 he published La formation des legends, his seventh book (reprinted in 1922). It was followed up by two books in 1911, Les Demi-savants 11 and volume III of Religions, mæurs et légendes. Essais d’ethnographie et de linguistique. He had by then become more and more engaged with general epistemological and methodological foundational issues. In 1913 in particular, he published a series of programmatic articles in which he denounced the problems in contemporary ‘scientific’ approaches, moving to propose an alternative platform for ethnographic practice and theory. 11 This highly original work was introduced and translated into English by Needham and published by Kegan and Paul in 1967 as The Semi-Scholars. The book is a humoristic and bitingly sarcastic description of the social sciences losing themselves in blindfolded specialization; after Rites of Passage in 1960 and The Semi-Scholars in 1967, no further work of van Gennep has been translated into English – regrettably. Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 39 In this period (1910–1911) he also carried out two rounds of ethnographic fieldwork in Algeria. Although the project to study art forms was only very partially successful (he mastered Arabic to some extent, but found it extremely difficult to communicate with the women who performed the art work he had wished to analyse), several lengthy publications followed from it, not least his 1914 book, En Algérie, which sums up some of his findings (for a contextual discussion of van Gennep’s fieldwork, see Siboud 2004 and Zerilli 1998b). Escape to Switzerland and Back Again Van Gennep certainly did not leave the inner circles of French anthropology without a fight, but it was becoming increasingly clear that he was not going to have an easy life in France. Following failed job applications at the Collège de France in 1907, 1909 and 1911, he decided to try abroad (Belmont 1979: 11; see again Chapter 2 for further detail). In 1912 he was offered the first (and only) academic position he ever held, as chair in ‘Swiss ethnography’ at the University of Neuchâtel. Switzerland remained the second-best choice, as doors in France kept closing. Geographically, if not also culturally (and certainly linguistically), it brought him quite close to his town of birth, Ludwigsburg. Henri Junod, the Swiss Protestant missionary and Tsonga expert, had first been appointed to the Chair but then stood down in favour of van Gennep. Junod was arguably the first scholar in the world to have recognized the importance of van Gennep’s Rites of Passage. Van Gennep’s concept of ritual passage served as the central analytical frame of volume I of Junod’s famous The Life of a South-African Tribe, published in Neuchâtel 1912 (Junod 1912). 12 Junod structured this famous monograph just like van Gennep had structured Rites of Passage, moving through the life cycle of an individual and accounting for the beliefs and practices associated with each phase of transition, from life to death (Junod makes a particularly interesting parallel analysis in the pointing out of the differences in ritual and symbolic form as differentiated by the male and female genders). Junod and van Gennep would come to know each other personally during the Neuchâtel years. This position could potentially have secured van Gennep a base from where he might have developed his academic project and a proper school of thought. It was not Paris, of course, but it was indeed a university: small but ambitious, centrally placed in Europe in a multi-lingual environment. The position in Switzerland had provided van Gennep an escape route from of French academia, but he had no intention of hiding away across the Alps; quite the contrary. He first re-organized the exhibitions of the museum in a way that reflected the cultural meanings of the displayed objects. He would publish many articles on museology throughout the rest of his life. Immediately upon his arrival he started to plan for a crucial event, which he clearly envisaged as a founding event for a new 12 It was by reading Junod that Victor Turner first heard of Arnold van Gennep, who was otherwise completely unknown in Anglo-Saxon anthropology before 1960. Liminality and the Modern 40 ethnographic/sociological science: the major international conference held at Neuchâtel in the summer of 1914, weeks before the outbreak of World War I. In these formative years of the social sciences, van Gennep saw an opportunity to mark the field, and to give it his imprint. Around 600 social scientists attended the event in 1914. Van Gennep’s intentions were clearly programmatic. The topics of debate concerned basic terminological and methodological issues, as well as attempts to delineate boundaries with neighbouring disciplines. The underlying questions of the entire conference were explicitly programmatic: What should our discipline look like? Which tasks should we set ourselves? And how are they to be carried out? Marcel Mauss was part of the French delegation, and gave himself a paper on taboo among the Baronga (Zerilli 1998a has the details). Durkheim did not attend, but we can be fairly sure that he received a detailed resumé of what had happened from Mauss. Under Durkheim’s guidance, Mauss was in that period preparing a plan for ethnographic studies in France, and certainly understood the importance of van Gennep’s project.
